label: Solstice
siteDescription: A fictional product-and-science notebook for showing the crisp, media-forward Solstice theme.
author: Kai Lin
about: Kai Lin Solar Lab is a fictional notebook about product interfaces, light, instruments, and solar imagery. The entries are invented for the demo and use public-domain NASA/SDO imagery as visual prompts, with sources cited in each article.

posts.title: First Light Interface
posts.excerpt: A clean product note using a public-domain SDO image to test Solstice's media treatment.

source.label: The Sun by NASA's Solar Dynamics Observatory on Wikimedia Commons
source.license: Public domain; created solely by NASA

body: A good interface should make first light feel immediate. Not dramatic, not mystical, just available.

Solstice is the right theme for this demo because it handles large media, code blocks, quotes, and metadata with a polished product surface. The image gets room, and the controls do not become the subject.
I would ship the first version with three states: quiet, tracking, and alert. Everything else can wait until the instrument earns more data.

posts.title: Filament Alert Prototype
posts.excerpt: A fictional product note on translating a solar filament image into a restrained alert panel.

source.label: SDO solar filament eruption image on Wikimedia Commons
source.license: Public domain; created solely by NASA

body: The prototype uses a single rule: the alert should explain the next action before it explains the phenomenon.
In the demo, I used Solstice's code and callout styling to check whether technical notes still read like product writing. The answer depends on restraint: one threshold, one chart, one decision.
A warning that cannot be acted on is just weather in a louder voice.

posts.title: Quiet Disk Mode
posts.excerpt: A display-mode sketch that uses a full solar disk as a focus object.

source.label: Sun disk, NASA/SDO on Wikimedia Commons
source.license: Public domain; created solely by NASA

body: Quiet disk mode is for rooms where nobody is actively debugging. It keeps the star visible and moves every number to the edge.
This article tests Solstice's ability to keep a circular image crisp against a minimal surface. The theme's radius, shadow, and media variables make the image feel placed rather than pasted.
The best status screen is the one people can understand while walking past it.

posts.title: Coronal Loop Dashboard
posts.excerpt: A dashboard sketch for coronal loop imagery and narrow operational copy.

source.label: SDO 2026 AIA 0171 coronal loops image on Wikimedia Commons
source.license: Public domain; created solely by NASA

body: The dashboard begins with a loop because loops are the easiest shape to over-explain. The interface has to resist that temptation.
I used this post to test tables, short paragraphs, and a source note after a high-contrast image. Solstice keeps the hierarchy clean enough for the article to stay readable.
A dashboard is not a wall of facts. It is a contract about which facts deserve the wall.
